nativeRange.habitat: Rocky littoral zone of northern Lake Tanganyika; associates closely with rock surfaces where it grazes aufwuchs (algal mats and associated micro-organisms). Found primarily in shallow to mid-depth rocky habitat.

decorPreferences: large piles of rockwork with many caves and crevices
open sandy areas between rock structures for territory delineation
minimal or no planting (disturbed by fish activity)
robust attached plants such as Anubias if used

diet.notes: Strictly herbivorous — feeds on aufwuchs (algal films and associated micro-organisms) in the wild. In the aquarium, spirulina-based flakes and pellets should form the core of the diet, supplemented with blanched spinach, nori (dried seaweed), and other vegetable matter. Protein-rich foods (beefheart, bloodworm, high-protein pellets) must be strictly avoided: the species has a long, specialised digestive tract adapted to low-protein plant material, and animal protein is a primary trigger for Tanganyikan bloat, which is rapidly fatal. Small, frequent feedings of plant-based foods are strongly preferred over large infrequent meals.

goodWith: Synodontis petricola and other Tanganyikan Synodontis catfish
Altolamprologus compressiceps (with caution, in very large tanks)
Julidochromis species (with caution, in very large tanks)
avoidWith: other Tropheus species or morphs (cross-morph mixing causes hybridisation; aggression surges with mixed groups)
Malawi cichlids or other non-Tanganyikan cichlids
small or slow-moving fish that cannot escape aggression
any fish that will compete for algae-grazing territory
fish requiring soft or acidic water

imageCandidates.notes: Adult specimen showing typical blue-black body with yellow/white lateral band (Maswa morph).

summary: An advanced-level Lake Tanganyika cichlid prized for a dramatic juvenile-to-adult colour transition: juveniles are jet-black with white polka dots; adults develop a blue-black body with a bright yellow or white lateral band. Strictly herbivorous and highly susceptible to Tanganyikan bloat if fed protein. Must be kept in large colonies of 15+ to distribute aggression.
careNotes:
Tropheus duboisi is endemic to the northern rocky shores of Lake Tanganyika and is one of the most challenging Rift Lake cichlids to keep successfully. It is emphatically not a fish for beginners. Several factors combine to make it demanding: extreme dietary sensitivity, high intraspecific aggression, and rigid water-quality requirements.

Water quality must be pristine and stable — hard, alkaline conditions (pH 8.0–9.0, gH 10–25, kH 6–15) mirroring Lake Tanganyika are non-negotiable. Temperature should be maintained at 23–27°C. Ammonia and nitrite must be zero; nitrate should be kept below 20 ppm. A high-capacity filter and regular water changes (20–30% weekly) are essential.

Diet is the single biggest killer of this species in captivity. The digestive system is highly adapted to low-protein aufwuchs grazing and cannot process animal protein safely. Spirulina flakes (high spirulina content, ideally 30%+), nori, blanched spinach, and other vegetable matter should form 100% of the diet. Beefheart, bloodworms, and high-protein pellets must never be fed; even a single feeding can trigger Tanganyikan bloat (internal bacterial infection / digestive blockage) which progresses rapidly to death. Small, frequent feedings (2–3 times daily) are preferred over large meals.

Conspecific aggression is intense, particularly among males. Contrary to intuition, larger groups (15–20+) reduce aggression by distributing it across many individuals — small groups of 5–6 often fight to the death. Maintain a ratio of approximately 6–10 females per male. The tank footprint should be at least 150 cm to allow multiple territories. A species-only or near-species-only setup is strongly recommended; tankmates should be limited to non-competing Tanganyikan species such as Synodontis catfish.

Juveniles are sold bearing the iconic black-with-white-polka-dot coloration; this gradually transitions to the adult pattern (blue-black body with a yellow or white lateral band) by 6–12 months. Multiple geographical colour morphs exist (Maswa, Kigoma, Bemba etc.) — do not mix morphs, as this causes hybridisation and spikes intraspecific aggression. See also: [tropheus-moorii] (fw-fish-534) — the closely related sister species sharing the same husbandry demands.
breedingNotes:
Maternal mouthbrooder. Spawning occurs in open water over rocks in typical Tanganyikan fashion; the female collects 5–15 eggs and broods them orally for approximately 4 weeks. Fry are large on release and can accept brine shrimp nauplii, microworms, and powdered spirulina foods immediately. The female continues limited brood care for a short period after first release, with fry returning to her mouth when threatened.

Breeding is difficult to achieve reliably. The primary challenge is male harassment: males seek to spawn constantly and will persistently pursue females, exhausting or killing them in small tanks. A large colony with many females per male, abundant rockwork cover, and a minimum tank of 300 L is needed to reduce harassment to manageable levels. Many breeders artificially strip fry from the female’s mouth at the two-week stage and raise them in a separate rearing tank to maximise yield and protect the female. Brood sizes are small (5–15 fry), making breeding less productive than most aquarium cichlids.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Tropheus duboisi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (12 cm), temperature (23–27°C), pH (8.0–9.5), hardness (8–25°H), minimum tank dimensions (60″ × 18″ × 18″ for groups of 15–20), diet (herbivore, no animal protein), breeding (maternal mouthbrooder, 5–15 eggs, ~4 weeks), grouping requirements (15–20 minimum), conspecific aggression pattern, juvenile colour phase, and bloat susceptibility.

additional.name: FishBase — Tropheus duboisi
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (12.0 cm TL), temperature (24–26°C), pH (8.5–9.0), hardness (dH 10–12), native distribution (northern Lake Tanganyika; DRC, Burundi, Tanzania), depth (to 30 m), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), diet (herbivorous aufwuchs grazer), breeding (maternal mouthbrooder, female guards fry briefly after release), IUCN status (Endangered EN, B2ab(iii), assessed March 2025), minimum aquarium length (150 cm).

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Tropheus: home is where the rocks are
additional.notes: Feature article on Tropheus genus husbandry. Confirmed colony-keeping requirements (10+ minimum, large species tank), male harassment as primary breeding challenge, Tanganyikan bloat risk from protein feeding, spirulina-based diet as standard of care, and artificial stripping technique for raising fry.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Tropheus Species Profiles
additional.notes: Additional hobbyist care reference. Confirmed pH 7.8–9.0, temperature 24–28°C, minimum 75 gal (284 L) for single specimen, 1.5 m+ tank length for colonies, dGH 10–15, kH 6+, nitrate below 20 ppm. Notes on robust plants (Anubias, Java fern), multiple geographical morphs, and strict no-mixing-of-morphs guidance.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; 5–15 eggs brooded ~4 weeks. Male harassment is the primary challenge — requires large colony (15+ fish) with high female-to-male ratio and abundant cover. Artificial stripping at 2 weeks is common practice to protect females and improve fry yield. Fry are large on release and accept brine shrimp nauplii and powdered spirulina immediately.
